146.play: Will you give thanks, sweet Kate; or else shall I?

150.play: PETRUCHIO: 'Tis burnt; and so is all the meat.

151.play: What dogs are these! Where is the rascal cook?

152.play: How durst you, villains, bring it from the dresser,

153.play: And serve it thus to me that love it not?

154.play: Theretake it to you, trenchers, cups, and all;

155.play: You heedless joltheads and unmanner'd slaves!

156.play: What, do you grumble? I'll be with you straight.

157.play: KATHARINA: I pray you, husband, be not so disquiet:

158.play: The meat was well, if you were so contented.

159.play: PETRUCHIO: I tell thee, Kate, 'twas burnt and dried away;

160.play: And I expressly am forbid to touch it,

161.play: For it engenders choler, planteth anger;

162.play: And better 'twere that both of us did fast,

163.play: Since, of ourselves, ourselves are choleric,

164.play: Than feed it with such over-roasted flesh.

165.play: Be patient; to-morrow 't shall be mended,

166.play: And, for this night, we'll fast for company:

167.play: Come, I will bring thee to thy bridal chamber.

168.play: NATHANIEL: Peter, didst ever see the like?

169.play: PETER: He kills her in her own humour.

171.play: CURTIS: In her chamber, making a sermon of continency to her;

172.play: And rails, and swears, and rates, that she, poor soul,

173.play: Knows not which way to stand, to look, to speak,

174.play: And sits as one new-risen from a dream.

175.play: Away, away! for he is coming hither.

176.play: PETRUCHIO: Thus have I politicly begun my reign,

177.play: And 'tis my hope to end successfully.

178.play: My falcon now is sharp and passing empty;

179.play: And till she stoop she must not be full-gorged,

180.play: For then she never looks upon her lure.

181.play: Another way I have to man my haggard,

182.play: To make her come and know her keeper's call,

183.play: That is, to watch her, as we watch these kites

184.play: That bate and beat and will not be obedient.

185.play: She eat no meat to-day, nor none shall eat;

186.play: Last night she slept not, nor to-night she shall not;

187.play: As with the meat, some undeserved fault

188.play: I'll find about the making of the bed;

189.play: And here I'll fling the pillow, there the bolster,

190.play: This way the coverlet, another way the sheets:

191.play: Ay, and amid this hurly I intend

192.play: That all is done in reverend care of her;

193.play: And in conclusion she shall watch all night:

194.play: And if she chance to nod I'll rail and brawl

195.play: And with the clamour keep her still awake.

196.play: This is a way to kill a wife with kindness;

197.play: And thus I'll curb her mad and headstrong humour.

198.play: He that knows better how to tame a shrew,

199.play: Now let him speak: 'tis charity to show.

2.play: TRANIO: Is't possible, friend Licio, that Mistress Bianca

3.play: Doth fancy any other but Lucentio?

4.play: I tell you, sir, she bears me fair in hand.

5.play: HORTENSIO: Sir, to satisfy you in what I have said,

6.play: Stand by and mark the manner of his teaching.

7.play: LUCENTIO: Now, mistress, profit you in what you read?

8.play: BIANCA: What, master, read you? first resolve me that.

9.play: LUCENTIO: I read that I profess, the Art to Love.

10.play: BIANCA: And may you prove, sir, master of your art!

11.play: LUCENTIO: While you, sweet dear, prove mistress of my heart!

12.play: HORTENSIO: Quick proceeders, marry! Now, tell me, I pray,

13.play: You that durst swear at your mistress Bianca

14.play: Loved none in the world so well as Lucentio.

15.play: TRANIO: O despiteful love! unconstant womankind!

16.play: I tell thee, Licio, this is wonderful.

17.play: HORTENSIO: Mistake no more: I am not Licio,

18.play: Nor a musician, as I seem to be;

19.play: But one that scorn to live in this disguise,

20.play: For such a one as leaves a gentleman,

21.play: And makes a god of such a cullion:

22.play: Know, sir, that I am call'd Hortensio.

23.play: TRANIO: Signior Hortensio, I have often heard

24.play: Of your entire affection to Bianca;

25.play: And since mine eyes are witness of her lightness,

26.play: I will with you, if you be so contented,

27.play: Forswear Bianca and her love for ever.

28.play: HORTENSIO: See, how they kiss and court! Signior Lucentio,

29.play: Here is my hand, and here I firmly vow

30.play: Never to woo her no more, but do forswear her,

31.play: As one unworthy all the former favours

32.play: That I have fondly flatter'd her withal.

33.play: TRANIO: And here I take the unfeigned oath,

34.play: Never to marry with her though she would entreat:

35.play: Fie on her! see, how beastly she doth court him!

36.play: HORTENSIO: Would all the world but he had quite forsworn!

37.play: For me, that I may surely keep mine oath,

38.play: I will be married to a wealthy widow,

39.play: Ere three days pass, which hath as long loved me

40.play: As I have loved this proud disdainful haggard.

42.play: Kindness in women, not their beauteous looks,

43.play: Shall win my love: and so I take my leave,

44.play: In resolution as I swore before.

45.play: TRANIO: Mistress Bianca, bless you with such grace

46.play: As 'longeth to a lover's blessed case!

47.play: Nay, I have ta'en you napping, gentle love,

48.play: And have forsworn you with Hortensio.

49.play: BIANCA: Tranio, you jest: but have you both forsworn me?

51.play: LUCENTIO: Then we are rid of Licio.

52.play: TRANIO: I' faith, he'll have a lusty widow now,

53.play: That shall be wood and wedded in a day.

55.play: TRANIO: Ay, and he'll tame her.

57.play: TRANIO: Faith, he is gone unto the taming-school.

58.play: BIANCA: The taming-school! what, is there such a place?

59.play: TRANIO: Ay, mistress, and Petruchio is the master;

60.play: That teacheth tricks eleven and twenty long,

61.play: To tame a shrew and charm her chattering tongue.

62.play: BIONDELLO: O master, master, I have watch'd so long

63.play: That I am dog-weary: but at last I spied

64.play: An ancient angel coming down the hill,

67.play: BIONDELLO: Master, a mercatante, or a pedant,

68.play: I know not what; but format in apparel,

69.play: In gait and countenance surely like a father.

70.play: LUCENTIO: And what of him, Tranio?

71.play: TRANIO: If he be credulous and trust my tale,

72.play: I'll make him glad to seem Vincentio,

73.play: And give assurance to Baptista Minola,

74.play: As if he were the right Vincentio

75.play: Take in your love, and then let me alone.

76.play: And you, sir! you are welcome.

77.play: Travel you far on, or are you at the farthest?

79.play: Of Mantua, sir? marry, God forbid!

80.play: And come to Padua, careless of your life?

81.play: 'Tis death for any one in Mantua

82.play: To come to Padua. Know you not the cause?

83.play: Your ships are stay'd at Venice, and the duke,

84.play: For private quarrel 'twixt your duke and him,

85.play: Hath publish'd and proclaim'd it openly:

86.play: 'Tis, marvel, but that you are but newly come,

87.play: You might have heard it else proclaim'd about.

88.play: Well, sir, to do you courtesy,

89.play: This will I do, and this I will advise you:

90.play: First, tell me, have you ever been at Pisa?

91.play: Among them know you one Vincentio?

92.play: He is my father, sir; and, sooth to say,

93.play: In countenance somewhat doth resemble you.

94.play: BIONDELLO: [Aside]  As much as an apple doth an oyster,

96.play: TRANIO: To save your life in this extremity,

97.play: This favour will I do you for his sake;

98.play: And think it not the worst of an your fortunes

99.play: That you are like to Sir Vincentio.

100.play: His name and credit shall you undertake,

101.play: And in my house you shall be friendly lodged:

102.play: Look that you take upon you as you should;

103.play: You understand me, sir: so shall you stay

104.play: Till you have done your business in the city:

105.play: If this be courtesy, sir, accept of it.

106.play: Then go with me to make the matter good.

107.play: This, by the way, I let you understand;

108.play: my father is here look'd for every day,

109.play: To pass assurance of a dower in marriage

110.play: 'Twixt me and one Baptista's daughter here:

111.play: In all these circumstances I'll instruct you:

112.play: Go with me to clothe you as becomes you.

2.play: GRUMIO: No, no, forsooth; I dare not for my life.

3.play: KATHARINA: The more my wrong, the more his spite appears:

4.play: What, did he marry me to famish me?

5.play: Beggars, that come unto my father's door,

6.play: Upon entreaty have a present aims;

7.play: If not, elsewhere they meet with charity:

8.play: But I, who never knew how to entreat,

9.play: Nor never needed that I should entreat,

10.play: Am starved for meat, giddy for lack of sleep,

11.play: With oath kept waking and with brawling fed:

12.play: And that which spites me more than all these wants,

13.play: He does it under name of perfect love;

14.play: As who should say, if I should sleep or eat,

15.play: 'Twere deadly sickness or else present death.

16.play: I prithee go and get me some repast;

17.play: I care not what, so it be wholesome food.

18.play: GRUMIO: What say you to a neat's foot?

19.play: KATHARINA: 'Tis passing good: I prithee let me have it.

20.play: GRUMIO: I fear it is too choleric a meat.

21.play: How say you to a fat tripe finely broil'd?

22.play: KATHARINA: I like it well: good Grumio, fetch it me.

23.play: GRUMIO: I cannot tell; I fear 'tis choleric.

24.play: What say you to a piece of beef and mustard?

25.play: KATHARINA: A dish that I do love to feed upon.

26.play: GRUMIO: Ay, but the mustard is too hot a little.

27.play: KATHARINA: Why then, the beef, and let the mustard rest.

28.play: GRUMIO: Nay then, I will not: you shall have the mustard,

29.play: Or else you get no beef of Grumio.

30.play: KATHARINA: Then both, or one, or any thing thou wilt.

31.play: GRUMIO: Why then, the mustard without the beef.

32.play: KATHARINA: Go, get thee gone, thou false deluding slave,

33.play: That feed'st me with the very name of meat:

34.play: Sorrow on thee and all the pack of you,

35.play: That triumph thus upon my misery!

36.play: Go, get thee gone, I say.

37.play: PETRUCHIO: How fares my Kate? What, sweeting, all amort?

39.play: KATHARINA: Faith, as cold as can be.

40.play: PETRUCHIO: Pluck up thy spirits; look cheerfully upon me.

41.play: Here love; thou see'st how diligent I am

42.play: To dress thy meat myself and bring it thee:

43.play: I am sure, sweet Kate, this kindness merits thanks.

44.play: What, not a word? Nay, then thou lovest it not;

45.play: And all my pains is sorted to no proof.

47.play: KATHARINA: I pray you, let it stand.

48.play: PETRUCHIO: The poorest service is repaid with thanks;

49.play: And so shall mine, before you touch the meat.

51.play: HORTENSIO: Signior Petruchio, fie! you are to blame.

52.play: Come, mistress Kate, I'll bear you company.

53.play: PETRUCHIO: [Aside]  Eat it up all, Hortensio, if thou lovest me.

54.play: Much good do it unto thy gentle heart!

55.play: Kate, eat apace: and now, my honey love,

56.play: Will we return unto thy father's house

57.play: And revel it as bravely as the best,

58.play: With silken coats and caps and golden rings,

59.play: With ruffs and cuffs and fardingales and things;

60.play: With scarfs and fans and double change of bravery,

61.play: With amber bracelets, beads and all this knavery.

62.play: What, hast thou dined? The tailor stays thy leisure,

63.play: To deck thy body with his ruffling treasure.

64.play: Come, tailor, let us see these ornaments;

67.play: Why, this was moulded on a porringer;

68.play: A velvet dish: fie, fie! 'tis lewd and filthy:

69.play: Why, 'tis a cockle or a walnut-shell,

70.play: A knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap:

71.play: Away with it! come, let me have a bigger.

72.play: KATHARINA: I'll have no bigger: this doth fit the time,

73.play: And gentlewomen wear such caps as these

74.play: PETRUCHIO: When you are gentle, you shall have one too,

76.play: HORTENSIO: [Aside]  That will not be in haste.

77.play: KATHARINA: Why, sir, I trust I may have leave to speak;

78.play: And speak I will; I am no child, no babe:

79.play: Your betters have endured me say my mind,

80.play: And if you cannot, best you stop your ears.

81.play: My tongue will tell the anger of my heart,

82.play: Or else my heart concealing it will break,

83.play: And rather than it shall, I will be free

84.play: Even to the uttermost, as I please, in words.

85.play: PETRUCHIO: Why, thou say'st true; it is a paltry cap,

86.play: A custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie:

87.play: I love thee well, in that thou likest it not.

88.play: KATHARINA: Love me or love me not, I like the cap;

89.play: And it I will have, or I will have none.

90.play: PETRUCHIO: Thy gown? why, ay: come, tailor, let us see't.

91.play: O mercy, God! what masquing stuff is here?

92.play: What's this? a sleeve? 'tis like a demi-cannon:

93.play: What, up and down, carved like an apple-tart?

94.play: Here's snip and nip and cut and slish and slash,

95.play: Like to a censer in a barber's shop:

96.play: Why, what, i' devil's name, tailor, call'st thou this?

97.play: HORTENSIO: [Aside]  I see she's like to have neither cap nor gown.

98.play: PETRUCHIO: Marry, and did; but if you be remember'd,

99.play: I did not bid you mar it to the time.

100.play: Go, hop me over every kennel home,

101.play: For you shall hop without my custom, sir:

102.play: I'll none of it: hence! make your best of it.

103.play: KATHARINA: I never saw a better-fashion'd gown,

104.play: More quaint, more pleasing, nor more commendable:

105.play: Belike you mean to make a puppet of me.

106.play: PETRUCHIO: Why, true; he means to make a puppet of thee.

107.play: O monstrous arrogance! Thou liest, thou thread,

109.play: Thou yard, three-quarters, half-yard, quarter, nail!

110.play: Thou flea, thou nit, thou winter-cricket thou!

111.play: Braved in mine own house with a skein of thread?

112.play: Away, thou rag, thou quantity, thou remnant;

113.play: Or I shall so be-mete thee with thy yard

114.play: As thou shalt think on prating whilst thou livest!

115.play: I tell thee, I, that thou hast marr'd her gown.

116.play: GRUMIO: I gave him no order; I gave him the stuff.

117.play: Marry, sir, with needle and thread.

119.play: Face not me: thou hast braved many men; brave not

120.play: me; I will neither be faced nor braved. I say unto

121.play: thee, I bid thy master cut out the gown; but I did

122.play: not bid him cut it to pieces: ergo, thou liest.

124.play: GRUMIO: The note lies in's throat, if he say I said so.

125.play: Master, if ever I said loose-bodied gown, sew me in

126.play: the skirts of it, and beat me to death with a bottom

127.play: of brown thread: I said a gown.

132.play: GRUMIO: Error i' the bill, sir; error i' the bill.

133.play: I commanded the sleeves should be cut out and

134.play: sewed up again; and that I'll prove upon thee,

135.play: though thy little finger be armed in a thimble.

136.play: I am for thee straight: take thou the

137.play: bill, give me thy mete-yard, and spare not me.

138.play: HORTENSIO: God-a-mercy, Grumio! then he shall have no odds.

139.play: PETRUCHIO: Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.

140.play: GRUMIO: You are i' the right, sir: 'tis for my mistress.

141.play: PETRUCHIO: Go, take it up unto thy master's use.

142.play: GRUMIO: Villain, not for thy life: take up my mistress'

144.play: PETRUCHIO: Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?

145.play: GRUMIO: O, sir, the conceit is deeper than you think for:

146.play: Take up my mistress' gown to his master's use!

148.play: PETRUCHIO: [Aside]  Hortensio, say thou wilt see the tailor paid.

149.play: Go take it hence; be gone, and say no more.

150.play: HORTENSIO: Tailor, I'll pay thee for thy gown tomorrow:

151.play: Take no unkindness of his hasty words:

152.play: Away! I say; commend me to thy master.

153.play: PETRUCHIO: Well, come, my Kate; we will unto your father's

154.play: Even in these honest mean habiliments:

155.play: Our purses shall be proud, our garments poor;

156.play: For 'tis the mind that makes the body rich;

157.play: And as the sun breaks through the darkest clouds,

158.play: So honour peereth in the meanest habit.

159.play: What is the jay more precious than the lark,

160.play: Because his fathers are more beautiful?

161.play: Or is the adder better than the eel,

162.play: Because his painted skin contents the eye?

163.play: O, no, good Kate; neither art thou the worse

164.play: For this poor furniture and mean array.

165.play: if thou account'st it shame. lay it on me;

166.play: And therefore frolic: we will hence forthwith,

167.play: To feast and sport us at thy father's house.

168.play: Go, call my men, and let us straight to him;

169.play: And bring our horses unto Long-lane end;

170.play: There will we mount, and thither walk on foot

171.play: Let's see; I think 'tis now some seven o'clock,

172.play: And well we may come there by dinner-time.

173.play: KATHARINA: I dare assure you, sir, 'tis almost two;

174.play: And 'twill be supper-time ere you come there.

175.play: PETRUCHIO: It shall be seven ere I go to horse:

176.play: Look, what I speak, or do, or think to do,

177.play: You are still crossing it. Sirs, let't alone:

178.play: I will not go to-day; and ere I do,

179.play: It shall be what o'clock I say it is.

180.play: HORTENSIO: [Aside]  Why, so this gallant will command the sun.

1.play: Enter TRANIO, and the Pedant dressed like VINCENTIO

2.play: TRANIO: Sir, this is the house: please it you that I call?

3.play: 'Tis well; and hold your own, in any case,

4.play: With such austerity as 'longeth to a father.

6.play: TRANIO: Fear you not him. Sirrah Biondello,

7.play: Now do your duty throughly, I advise you:

10.play: TRANIO: But hast thou done thy errand to Baptista?

11.play: BIONDELLO: I told him that your father was at Venice,

12.play: And that you look'd for him this day in Padua.

13.play: TRANIO: Thou'rt a tall fellow: hold thee that to drink.

14.play: Here comes Baptista: set your countenance, sir.

15.play: Signior Baptista, you are happily met.

16.play: Sir, this is the gentleman I told you of:

17.play: I pray you stand good father to me now,

18.play: Give me Bianca for my patrimony.

19.play: BAPTISTA: Sir, pardon me in what I have to say:

20.play: Your plainness and your shortness please me well.

21.play: Right true it is, your son Lucentio here

22.play: Doth love my daughter and she loveth him,

23.play: Or both dissemble deeply their affections:

24.play: And therefore, if you say no more than this,

25.play: That like a father you will deal with him

26.play: And pass my daughter a sufficient dower,

27.play: The match is made, and all is done:

28.play: Your son shall have my daughter with consent.

29.play: TRANIO: I thank you, sir. Where then do you know best

30.play: We be affied and such assurance ta'en

31.play: As shall with either part's agreement stand?

32.play: BAPTISTA: Not in my house, Lucentio; for, you know,

33.play: Pitchers have ears, and I have many servants:

34.play: Besides, old Gremio is hearkening still;

35.play: And happily we might be interrupted.

36.play: TRANIO: Then at my lodging, an it like you:

37.play: There doth my father lie; and there, this night,

38.play: We'll pass the business privately and well.

39.play: Send for your daughter by your servant here:

40.play: My boy shall fetch the scrivener presently.

41.play: The worst is this, that, at so slender warning,

42.play: You are like to have a thin and slender pittance.

43.play: BAPTISTA: It likes me well. Biondello, hie you home,

44.play: And bid Bianca make her ready straight;

45.play: And, if you will, tell what hath happened,

46.play: Lucentio's father is arrived in Padua,

47.play: And how she's like to be Lucentio's wife.

48.play: BIONDELLO: I pray the gods she may with all my heart!

49.play: TRANIO: Dally not with the gods, but get thee gone.

50.play: Signior Baptista, shall I lead the way?

51.play: Welcome! one mess is like to be your cheer:

52.play: Come, sir; we will better it in Pisa.

59.play: expound the meaning or moral of his signs and tokens.

1.play: Enter PETRUCHIO, KATHARINA, HORTENSIO, and Servants

2.play: PETRUCHIO: Come on, i' God's name; once more toward our father's.

3.play: Good Lord, how bright and goodly shines the moon!

4.play: KATHARINA: The moon! the sun: it is not moonlight now.

5.play: PETRUCHIO: I say it is the moon that shines so bright.

6.play: KATHARINA: I know it is the sun that shines so bright.

7.play: PETRUCHIO: Now, by my mother's son, and that's myself,

8.play: It shall be moon, or star, or what I list,

9.play: Or ere I journey to your father's house.

10.play: Go on, and fetch our horses back again.

11.play: Evermore cross'd and cross'd; nothing but cross'd!

12.play: HORTENSIO: Say as he says, or we shall never go.

13.play: KATHARINA: Forward, I pray, since we have come so far,

14.play: And be it moon, or sun, or what you please:

15.play: An if you please to call it a rush-candle,

16.play: Henceforth I vow it shall be so for me.

17.play: PETRUCHIO: I say it is the moon.

18.play: KATHARINA: I know it is the moon.

19.play: PETRUCHIO: Nay, then you lie: it is the blessed sun.

20.play: KATHARINA: Then, God be bless'd, it is the blessed sun:

21.play: But sun it is not, when you say it is not;

22.play: And the moon changes even as your mind.

23.play: What you will have it named, even that it is;

24.play: And so it shall be so for Katharina.

25.play: HORTENSIO: Petruchio, go thy ways; the field is won.

26.play: PETRUCHIO: Well, forward, forward! thus the bowl should run,

27.play: And not unluckily against the bias.

28.play: But, soft! company is coming here.

29.play: Good morrow, gentle mistress: where away?

30.play: Tell me, sweet Kate, and tell me truly too,

31.play: Hast thou beheld a fresher gentlewoman?

32.play: Such war of white and red within her cheeks!

33.play: What stars do spangle heaven with such beauty,

34.play: As those two eyes become that heavenly face?

35.play: Fair lovely maid, once more good day to thee.

36.play: Sweet Kate, embrace her for her beauty's sake.

37.play: HORTENSIO: A' will make the man mad, to make a woman of him.
